That registration secures the Act's fiscal incentives, the expropriation and transfer guarantees, and an automatic expatriate quota scaled to capital — but it is entirely distinct from, and does not substitute for, any sector-specific operating licence, which section 1(2) and section 30(c) expressly preserve as a separate and cumulative requirement to be met before the regulated service may lawfully be provided.

*One matter turns on facts not supplied and would change the advice: the identity of the service and its governing regulator. Whether a sector licence is required at all, and on what terms, is answered by the specific sectoral enactment for that service — not by Act 865 — so that instrument should be identified and applied alongside the GIPC analysis above.*

answers.q: A company wishes to abstract water from a river to support its mining operations. Advise on what authorisation is required, who grants it, what conditions may attach, and whether the authorising body has a discretion to refuse.
answers.answer:
# Advice: Authorisation to Abstract Water for Mining Operations

## The governing framework and the double-permission problem

The starting point is that a mineral right does not, of itself, carry the right to take water. Under the Minerals and Mining Act, 2006 (Act 703), the water entitlement is expressly made conditional.Section 17 provides that "subject to obtaining the requisite approvals or licences under the Water Resources Commission Act 1996 (Act 522), a holder of a mineral right may, for purposes of or ancillary to the mineral operations, obtain, divert, impound, convey and use water from a river, stream,"underground reservoir or watercourse within the land the subject of the mineral right.

The operative words carry the whole point of this advice. The phrase "subject to obtaining the requisite approvals or licences under the Water Resources Commission Act" is a condition precedent: the client's mineral right permits it to use river water only *after* a separate water authorisation has been obtained under Act 522. Two permissions therefore stand between the client and the river — the mineral right under Act 703, and the water permit under Act 522 — and they are granted by different bodies under different statutes. As the commentary in the materials confirms,where the proposed mineral operations is likely to use water in its natural state and/or affect water resources, the holder must also obtain a water right/use permit from the Water Resources Commission.## What authorisation is required, and who grants it

The authorisation the client needs is a **water use permit from the Water Resources Commission (WRC)**. The WRC is the lead agency for the resource:the long title of the Water Resources Commission Act describes it as "An Act to establish a Water Resources Commission, to provide for its composition and functions on the regulation and management of the utilisation of water resources in Ghana and for related matters".More concretely for the client,the holder of a mineral right must obtain the requisite approvals or licenses from the Water Resources Commission before it may "obtain, divert, impound, convey and use water from a river, stream, underground reservoir or watercourse within the land the subject of the mineral right"; and although the Commission has the legislative right to grant those licences, the management of water use in mineral operations rests on an integrative approach imposing obligations on a number of government agencies.The **procedure** is not governed by Act 522 in the abstract but by subsidiary legislation made under it.The process of acquiring water rights for mining activities is governed by the Water Use Regulations, 2001 (L.I. 1692) and the Drilling Licence and Groundwater Development Regulations 2006 (L.I. 1827); these regulations were promulgated pursuant to section 35 of the Water Resources Commission Act, and the Water Use Regulations detail the specific administrative procedures a mineral rights holder must go through to obtain a water permit for purposes of exercising mineral rights.On these facts the client wishes to take water **from a river**, so the instrument that governs its application is the Water Use Regulations, 2001 (L.I. 1692). The Drilling Licence and Groundwater Development Regulations, 2006 (L.I. 1827) govern a different activity — the sinking of wells to abstract *groundwater* — and would engage only if the client also intended to abstract from beneath the surface. That distinction matters:under L.I. 1827 a person shall not construct a well for the abstraction, or monitoring of groundwater or for research without a drilling licence granted in accordance with those Regulations, and the drilling licence must be obtained from the Commission on application.River abstraction and groundwater drilling are separate authorisations; the client's stated plan (river abstraction) engages the former, and it is the WRC that grants both.

A practical point on the *sequencing* of the two permissions. Although section 17 of Act 703 makes the water approval a precondition to *exercising* the water entitlement, the integrative practice described in the materials allows the requirement to be built into the mineral-right grant itself.If by the nature of the application the applicant must obtain a water use license, the Water Resources Commission can indicate such requirement so that the grant of the mineral right by the Minister will be subject to a water use license obtained from the Water Resources Commission.The client should therefore expect the water permit to be treated not as an optional add-on but as a condition tied to its right to mine at all.

## What conditions may attach

The permit is not an unconditional licence to take as much water as the operation needs; it is a regulated and revocable authorisation, and several kinds of condition may attach.

First, the permit fixes the **quantity and manner** of abstraction and is enforced through metering. The offence provision in the Water Use Regulations makes this concrete:under Regulation 24 of the Water Use Regulations, a permit holder who interferes with, or permits a person to interfere with, a controlling or measuring device so that the quantity of water abstracted, diverted or stored is not in accordance with the terms of the holder's permit or with an order of the Commission commits an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding two hundred and fifty penalty units or to imprisonment not exceeding twelve months, or both.The client should anticipate a permitted volume, a requirement to install and maintain measuring devices, and criminal exposure for taking outside the permitted terms.

Second, the permit may be **suspended or cancelled**.The water rights granted under the Water Resources Commission Act can be suspended or cancelled for improper use.The authorisation is therefore ongoing and conditional in nature, not a one-off entitlement.

Third, the water permit sits inside a wider web of **environmental and forestry approvals** that condition the mining operation as a whole, and the WRC does not act in isolation. Before undertaking operations,before undertaking an activity or operation under a mineral right, the holder of the mineral right shall obtain the necessary approvals and permits required from the Forestry Commission and the Environmental Protection Agency for the protection of natural resources, public health and the environment.The EPA holds a particularly powerful lever over the water permit:when the EPA requires the holder to submit an environmental impact assessment, it shall inform the Minerals Commission and any government agency which has responsibility to issue a licence, permit, approval or consent for mineral operations to withhold that licence, permit, approval or consent until the EPA informs the agency that the mandates under the environmental impact assessment have been complied with.In practical terms, the WRC's water permit can be held back at the EPA's instance pending EIA compliance — so the client's timetable for water abstraction is hostage to the environmental process running in parallel.

## Does the WRC have a discretion to refuse?

Yes. The authorisation is discretionary, not an entitlement the client can demand once it holds a mineral right, and the discretion is exercised on substantive resource-management grounds rather than as a rubber stamp.

The clearest expression of this is the integrative permitting practice the materials describe. When an application is processed, the WRC's representative assesses the effect of the proposed operation on water bodies, and the outcome can be adverse to the applicant.The representative of the Water Resources Commission is able to advise the committee as to the effect of the proposed mineral operations on water bodies in and around the mining area; and if the proposed mining operation will adversely affect water resources for which there can be no appreciable remedial action to reduce or minimise the adverse effect, the Minerals Commission may advise the Minister to exercise his power under section 4 of the Minerals and Mining Act to set the land aside from mining for being an ecologically fragile area.That is the strongest form of refusal — not merely declining the water permit, but recommending that the land be excluded from mining altogether. It follows that a permit is capable of being refused where the impact on the resource is serious and irremediable.

The discretion is also structured by a sustainability criterion built into the permitting approach.A working-group style approach to permitting ensures that the Commission will, among other things, critically consider issues of sustainable water use and general environmental management when deciding on the issuance of permits.Refusal or conditioning is therefore anchored in a legitimate purpose — protecting the resource — which is exactly the sort of consideration a court would expect the Commission to weigh.

That said, the discretion is not unfettered, and the client is not without protection. The Commission must exercise its power for the statutory purpose of managing and regulating the utilisation of water resources; a refusal on grounds unrelated to that purpose, or one that ignores the availability of remedial measures, would be vulnerable to challenge. The materials themselves signal that the discretion is exercised against a factual assessment of impact and of whether "appreciable remedial action" exists — implying that where impact can be mitigated, outright refusal is harder to justify.

**A note on the international dimension.** The scenario concerns abstraction from *a river*. If the river in question forms part of an international basin governed by a treaty regime, a further layer of authorisation logic applies. The Volta Basin Water Charter, for instance, subjects abstractions to a permit-or-declaration system:abstractions and the utilisation of non-abstracted quantities of water in the Basin, whatever the purpose, shall be subject to either prior authorization or prior declaration.Under that regime the authorising decision is expressly resource-constrained —authorization shall take account of the availability of water resources and the intended use, and shall be issued with due concern for maintaining target flows— which reinforces the point that abstraction from a shared river can be refused or curtailed to protect basin-wide flows. On the facts as given, the domestic WRC permit is the operative authorisation; the treaty regime would bite only if the river is a transboundary watercourse within such a Charter's scope, a matter I address below.

## Conclusion and recommended steps

The client must obtain a **water use permit from the Water Resources Commission** under Act 522, following the procedure in the Water Use Regulations, 2001 (L.I. 1692). This is a condition precedent to exercising the water entitlement that section 17 of Act 703 attaches to its mineral right, and the WRC has a genuine discretion to refuse or to grant subject to conditions — metered quantity limits, sustainability requirements, and liability to suspension or cancellation for improper use. The permit does not stand alone: the EPA can force it to be withheld pending EIA compliance, and severe irremediable impact on the resource can lead to the land being set aside from mining altogether. The client should treat the water permit as an integral gating condition on the whole project, not a formality, and should engage the WRC, EPA, Forestry Commission and Minerals Commission early and in parallel given the integrated permitting practice.

## Additional Facts Material to the Advice

Two open facts would materially change this advice:

1. **Whether the river is a transboundary watercourse within a treaty basin regime (e.g. the Volta Basin).** If it is, the abstraction attracts not only the domestic WRC permit but the Charter's prior-authorisation-or-declaration system, under which the authorising body must weigh basin-wide availability and target flows and may curtail the abstraction accordingly. This adds a distinct constraint and a further ground for refusal beyond the domestic assessment.

2. **Whether the client also intends to abstract groundwater (by sinking wells) as well as taking river water.** If so, a separate drilling licence under L.I. 1827 is required in addition to the surface-water permit, with its own application requirements and its own prohibition on constructing a well without a licence. On the facts as stated (river abstraction), only the Water Use Regulations regime is engaged, but the point should be confirmed because most mining operations draw on both sources.

answers.course: REGULATION OF TREE AND FOREST RESERVES
answers.q: A developer wishes to fell trees within a forest reserve to build an access road. Advise on what permissions are required, who may grant them, and whether felling may begin before any required permission is obtained.
answers.answer:
## The developer's position in short

Felling any tree in a forest reserve without the written consent of the competent forest authority is a criminal offence, so the developer must obtain that consent before a single tree is touched. The instrument that fits this scenario best is a **Salvage Permit**, granted by the Chief Executive of the Forestry Commission, because the road is a development that will require the removal of trees from the reserve. Felling may **not** begin before the permission is in hand — commencement without it is precisely what the Forest Protection legislation punishes.

## Why the reserve setting is decisive

The starting point is where the trees stand. The materials draw a sharp line between the "off-reserve" farm landscape and the gazetted forest reserves that make up the permanent forest estate. Because the developer wants to fell **inside a reserve**, the elaborate owner-consent machinery that governs off-reserve grants — the twenty-one-day notice to owners, the written consent under regulation 5, and the objection procedure before an ad hoc committee under regulation 6 — is not the operative route here. Those provisions are expressly directed at "lands other than public land and existing forest reserves";the publication-to-obtain-consent procedure applies where the land identified is not public land or forest reserve. In a reserve the controlling constraint is instead the criminal prohibition on unauthorised felling.

## The governing prohibition — felling in a reserve without consent is an offence

The operative rule is in the Forest Protection legislation.The Forest Protection Decree 1974 (NRCD 243), as amended by the Forest Protection (Amendment) Act 2002 (Act 624), provides that any person who, in a Forest Reserve without the written consent of the competent forest authority, fells, uproots, lops, girdles or otherwise damages any tree or timbercommits a forest offence — and the same subsection catches a person whomakes or cultivates any farm or erects any buildingin the reserve. Road-building for an access route is exactly the kind of construction-driven clearance the section reaches: felling trees and erecting works in a reserve, both without written consent, fall squarely within the prohibited acts.

The two limbs to note are that the consent must be **written** and must come from the **competent forest authority**. Neither an oral nod nor a landowner's agreement satisfies the section; the authority whose consent counts is the forest authority, not the stool or the farmer.

## Which permission fits — the Salvage Permit

Among the harvesting rights in the materials, the Salvage Permit is the instrument designed for this situation.A salvage permit under Regulation 29 is granted for the removal of trees from areas of land undergoing approved development such as road construction, expansion of human settlement and cultivation of farms.An access road is the paradigm case. The rationale the materials give for the permit also matches:its objectives are to prevent the destruction and wastage of trees in areas under development and to raise revenue to support forest management operations.One qualification bears directly on the developer.The area should not be subject to a Timber Utilization Contract (TUC), except where the applicant is the TUC holder.So if the stretch of reserve the road will cross is already under a subsisting TUC held by someone else, a salvage permit cannot simply be issued over it, and the conflict would have to be resolved first. That is a fact worth pinning down before the developer applies.

## Who grants it, and by what procedure

The grant lies with the **Chief Executive of the Forestry Commission**, on a verified report. The procedure the materials describe runs as follows:on receipt of the application, the Chief Executive refers it through the Executive Director of the Forest Services Division to the respective Regional and District Managers in the area where the permit is sought, for verification reports.Thena favourable report will attract a grant or approval by the Chief Executive, andthe terms and conditions for the grant are stated in the permit. Once granted,timber harvested under such a permit can be exported or sold on the domestic market— which matters if the felled logs have commercial value the developer wishes to realise rather than waste.

The practical sequence, then, is: application to the Chief Executive → referral through the FSD Executive Director to Regional and District Managers → verification report → favourable report → grant by the Chief Executive, with conditions specified in the permit itself.

## Can felling begin before the permission issues? No.

This is the sharpest point, and the answer is unambiguous. The Forest Protection legislation makes the **written consent a precondition** of lawful felling, not a formality to be regularised afterwards. Felling first and seeking permission later does not cure the illegality; it *is* the offence. On the plain words of the amended section 1, the moment the developer fells a tree in the reserve without the competent authority's written consent, the offence is complete.

The consequences are serious and are set out in the materials.A person who fells, harvests, girdles or in any way causes damage or injury to a tree in a forest reserve without written authorisation faces a maximum fine of 500 penalty units or 2 years imprisonment or both, and for a second or subsequent offence a fine of not less than 250 penalty units or a maximum of 3 years imprisonment or both.The same penalty band attaches to erecting buildings or structures and to obstructing river channels in a reserve — relevant because road construction may well involve both.

There is a further structural reason the developer cannot self-authorise. The permit regime builds verification *into* the grant: the Regional and District Managers must inspect and report favourably before the Chief Executive acts. Commencing before that report defeats the very control the permit exists to impose — the assessment of what may be taken, on what terms, and at what stumpage. Felling in advance forecloses the stumpage measurement and fee calculation that ordinarily precede lawful lifting of timber under the harvesting regime. The lawful order is permission first, felling second.

## Advice

The developer should make a written application for a Salvage Permit under Regulation 29 to the Chief Executive of the Forestry Commission, identifying the reserve and the road corridor, and await the verification reports and the Chief Executive's grant before any clearing begins. He must first confirm that the corridor is not subject to a TUC held by another party; if it is, that conflict must be resolved before a salvage permit can issue. Under no circumstances should trees be felled ahead of the grant: doing so is a forest offence carrying a fine of up to 500 penalty units or two years' imprisonment or both, with heavier exposure on repetition, and the illegality cannot be retrospectively cured. Permission first; felling only once the permit — and its stated terms and conditions — is in hand.

Two matters would sharpen this advice: first, whether the road corridor is already covered by any subsisting Timber Utilization Contract (which would bar a salvage permit unless the developer is itself the holder); and second, whether the development has been formally "approved" in the sense Regulation 29 contemplates, since the salvage route presupposes an approved development such as road construction — if there is no such approval, the appropriate authorisation to remove trees from the reserve may need to be reconsidered.
